theory.intro: A graph can give a false impression even when its numbers are correct. Spotting misleading features helps you judge data fairly.
facts: A **broken (truncated) axis** that does not start at $0$ makes small differences look huge.
**Uneven scales** or different bar widths can exaggerate or hide a change.
Always read the actual numbers, not just how big the bars *look*.
steps: Check where the vertical axis starts — is it $0$, or higher?
Check the scale is even and the bars are the same width.
Compare the real values to decide if the picture is fair.

foundation.q: This column graph's vertical axis starts at $90$, not $0$. What makes it misleading?
foundation.a: the axis does not start at $0$, so the gap looks bigger than it is

foundation.q: Using the same graph, what are the real values of $A$ and $B$?
Does this truncated-axis graph make $B$ look much bigger or only a little bigger than $A$?
A graph starts its axis at $0$ and the bars are equal width. Is this graph misleading?
In this graph the bars are different widths. Why is that misleading?
foundation.a: a wider bar looks bigger even at the same height

foundation.q: A pictograph uses bigger and bigger icons but each still means the same number. Is that fair?
This graph's vertical axis starts at $0$. The two bars are $40$ and $44$. Do they look very different?
Which axis feature is the warning sign of a misleading column graph: starting at $0$, or starting above $0$?
A graph's axis jumps $0, 10, 50, 60$ between marks. Why is that misleading?
foundation.a: the scale is uneven, so heights cannot be compared fairly

foundation.q: True or false: a graph can have correct numbers but still be misleading.

development.q: Two shops report sales $\$102$ and $\$108$. A graph with axis from $100$ makes shop $2$ look double. What is the real difference?
Using that graph, redrawn with the axis from $0$, would the two bars look very different?
A graph shows support 'rocketing' from $48\%$ to $52\%$ using an axis from $45\%$. By how many percentage points did support really change?
A line graph uses a squashed time axis to make a rise look steep. What should you check to judge the real change?
development.a: the actual values on the vertical axis

development.q: A column graph has axis from $0$ but one bar is drawn $3$D and forward, looking taller. Why is that misleading?
development.a: the 3D effect makes that bar look bigger than its value

development.q: A report claims a 'huge jump' in members from $300$ to $310$. On a $0$-based axis, is that jump big or small?
A truncated graph shows test averages $78$ and $80$ as bars of very different heights. State the real difference and whether the graph is fair.
A graph compares two years but one bar covers $12$ months and the other only $6$. Why is comparing the totals unfair?
development.a: the time periods are different lengths

development.q: A pictograph shows $\bullet = 100$ but the last symbol is only half drawn while labelled as a full $100$. What is misleading?
development.a: a half symbol is being counted as a whole

development.q: A line graph's vertical axis starts at $95$. Sales went $96, 97, 98$. Does the line look like a steep climb or a gentle rise?
development.a: looks steep, but it is a gentle rise

mastery.q: A graph with axis from $90$ shows bars at $92$ and $96$, making $B$ look twice $A$. Explain in one sentence why this is misleading and give the true difference.
mastery.a: the axis is truncated; the real difference is only $4$

mastery.q: Two charts show the same data: chart $1$ axis from $0$, chart $2$ axis from $80$. Which would a person use to exaggerate a small lead, and why?
mastery.a: chart $2$, because the truncated axis magnifies small gaps

mastery.q: A bar graph shows $\$50$ and $\$55$ but draws the second bar three times taller by starting the axis at $\$49$. What honest change should the axis show?
An advert claims 'twice as effective' and shows a bar twice as tall. The values are $80\%$ and $84\%$ on an axis from $76\%$. Is the claim justified?
mastery.a: no — it only rose by $4$ points

mastery.q: A graph leaves out one low data value so the average looks higher. What is this kind of bias called, in simple terms?
A truncated graph shows attendances $200$ and $210$ as bars of ratio $1:3$. State the real ratio (simplest form is fine) and whether the picture is honest.
A line graph plots months unevenly (Jan, Feb, then jumps to Dec) on equal spacing. Why does the trend look misleading?
mastery.a: equal spacing for unequal time gaps distorts the trend

mastery.q: Sketch-in-words: how would you redraw a misleading truncated bar graph to make it fair?
mastery.a: start the vertical axis at $0$ with an even scale

mastery.q: A graph shows profit bars at $20$ and $40$ on a $0$ axis, but the $40$ bar is twice as wide as well as twice as tall. Why does it overstate the rise?
mastery.a: doubling both width and height makes the area look four times bigger

mastery.q: Explain why reading the numbers, not just the bar heights, protects you from a misleading graph.
mastery.a: the numbers give the true values whatever the drawing suggests
